overview.description: Component for displaying a link in a button style.
overview.purpose: Component for displaying a link in a button style.
whenToUse: When you need a button that looks like a link.
whenNotToUse: When you need a standard button.

examples.basic: <mc-link-button href="https://designsystem.maersk.com/support/" label="Link" fit="medium" icon="" trailingicon="" appearance="primary" variant="filled" padding="default" width="auto" justifyitems="center" rel="" target="" arialabel="" ariacurrent="" ariarole=""></mc-link-button>

advanced.WithRouterLink:
import '@maersk-global/mds-components-core/mc-link-button';

<mc-link-button>
  <router-link to="/my-route">Link button with router link</router-link>
</mc-link-button>

Vue3.basic:
<mc-link-button
  href="https://designsystem.maersk.com/support/"
  label="Link"
  hiddenlabel
  fit="small"
  icon="airplane"
  trailingicon="airplane"
  loading
  appearance="secondary"
  variant="outlined"
  padding="compact"
  width="full-width"
  rounded
  justifyitems="left"
  disabled
  active
  rel="value"
  target="value"
  arialabel="value"
  ariacurrent="value"
  ariarole="value"
  @click="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @focus="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @blur="yourEventHandler(e)">
</mc-link-button>

<script type="module"
  src="./node_modules/@maersk-global/mds-components-core-link-button/index.js"></script>

<script>
  document.querySelector('mc-link-button').addEventListener('click', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-link-button').addEventListener('focus', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-link-button').addEventListener('blur', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
</script>

props.name: href
props.description: Passing a href value will render the button as a HTML anchor element.

props.name: label
props.description: Label can be passed as a simple argument like: `label="Label"` or as a slot to mc-link-button. Use argument style for passing short labels, use slot when you want to pass label with HTML text. Label is required attribute as it will be used as aria-label.

props.name: hiddenlabel
props.description: If set to `true` or the attribute is just presented without any value, then the label will not appear, but will be used as `aria-label` only

props.name: fit
props.description: Description missing

props.name: icon
props.description: Name of the icon

props.name: trailingicon
props.description: Name of the icon

props.name: loading
props.description: If set to `true`, it'll set the button state to loading and a loading spinner will be shown. The leading/trailing icon (if exists) is replaced by the spinner. This state also prevents the user from clicking and activating or submitting something more than one time.

props.name: appearance
props.description: If not specified, the default value is: primary

props.name: variant
props.description: Description missing

props.name: padding
props.description: Adjusts the padding size.

props.name: width
props.description: Auto width style depends on the text length, full-width style will adjust to the parent container width

props.name: rounded
props.description: Whether the button should have fully rounded corners.

props.name: justifyitems
props.description: Allows to position items inside the button like label text & icon. Specially useful when using `width="full-width"`

props.name: disabled
props.description: Description missing

props.name: active
props.description: Description missing

props.name: rel
props.description: The relationship of the linked URL as space-separated link types.

props.name: target
props.description: Where to display the linked URL, as the name for a browsing context (a tab, window, or <iframe>).

props.name: arialabel
props.description: Should 'only' be used if for the sake of accessibility the `aria-label` is differnt that the label which appears on the button, so that for instance the screen reader can provide more details than what's being visually displayed as a label on the button component.

props.name: ariacurrent
props.description: Sets the aria-current on the inner button in cases like pagination where the button is repeated in a list and at the same time, the currently active item needs to be specified.

props.name: ariarole
props.description: Sets the `role` on the inner button in cases like `tab-bar` where of every button is `tab`

events.name: click
events.description: Emitted when the button is clicked.

events.name: focus
events.description: Emitted when the button is focused.

events.name: blur
events.description: Emitted when the button is blurred.

slots.description: Use the default slot, when you want to pass the label as HTML. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
Use the icon named slot, if a custom graphic, pictogram, etc. should be used, instead of using MDS icons. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: icon

slots.description: Use the trailingicon named slot, if a custom graphic, pictogram, etc. should be used, instead of using MDS icons. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: trailingicon

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the button element inside the mc-link-button component. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: button

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the icon element inside the mc-link-button component. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: icon

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the container element inside the component. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: text-and-icon

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the text element inside the component. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: text-and-icon-labels

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the label element inside the component. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: text-and-icon-label

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the sublabel element inside the component. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: text-and-icon-sublabel

guidelines.title: Link Button
guidelines.description: A link button combines the visual appearance of a button with the navigational functionality of a hyperlink.

best-practice: Use link buttons for navigation within an app or website and other URL-change actions.
Link buttons can be used for important <PageLink path="/components/link/#stand-alone">stand-alone links</PageLink> in the UI, e.g. a “Register” link.
Use clear and descriptive labels so the user understands the destination of the link.
The purpose of the link button should be understandable from the label alone.
Avoid ambiguous and vague labels that are open to interpretation.
Always make sure that the link button is the best-suited option.
usage: While <PageLink path="/components/button">regular buttons</PageLink> typically are used for on-page actions, link buttons are used for navigating within an app or website.
primary: The link button can be used to highlight the most important links that lead the user forward through the main user journeys of the website or application.
secondary: The link button can also be used for less critical, but still important links that sometimes have a CTA-quality to them. These buttons should be less visually prominent and it is recommended to use the outlined button style for these.
in-page-url-changes: The link button can also be used for in-page navigation, where the link button links to a specific section within the page. Examples of this could be a table of contents or showing/hiding specific sections of the page.
icons: Like the <PageLink path="/components/button">regular buttons</PageLink>, the link button can contain an icon, either one to the left or one to the right side of the button label. It is also possible to have an icon-only button.

designPrinciples.title: Colour roles
designPrinciples.description: "Understand our different colour roles, the meaning behind their names, and how to use them."

primary: This is our main UI colour and will be the one that is reflected in the primary buttons, checkboxes, radio buttons, switches etc. meaning it will be the most prominent colour in many components and in your UI together with the neutral tones.
secondary: This is also referred to as the "brand" colour and will be reflected in the secondary button. This will be used to highlight the brand, while it's labelled as secondary, it can serve as the primary action. However, it's important not to overuse it. Use this colour to draw attention, use it for that “big primary” search button.
neutral: Neutral colours are present in nearly all components and serve as the foundation for any user interface. They are used in different aspects of a user interface like backgrounds, borders, and text colours. This palette contains both white and black, as well as all shades in between.
error: This refers to a shade of red, its main purpose is to communicate and highlight something "negative." For example, it indicates that you are performing a dangerous action, such as deleting your profile. However, it can also be used in other situations, such as displaying that a profile is busy, has unread notifications, stock values have decreased, you have an unpaid bill or you typed something wrong in an input field.
success: This refers to a shade of green, its main purpose is to communicate and highlight something "positive." For example, that your file was successfully uploaded or that your profile was updated. But it can also be used for ex. displaying an online/available status or that numbers have increased.
warning: This refers to a shade of yellow, its main purpose is to communicate and highlight a potential problem. For example, it may indicate that you have lost connection to the server or that your payment details need to be updated by a certain date. It can also be used for e.g. to display an "away" profile or a pending status.
info: This refers to a shade of blue, and its main purpose is to help and inform the user. For example, it can highlight a related feature that may be beneficial for the user to know about or to indicate that a new version is available. It can also be used for e.g. to signal that the user is currently on a "free" plan.

relatedGuidelines.title: Accessibility
relatedGuidelines.description: Improving the user experience for everyone.

what-is-accessibility?: Accessibility means that websites, tools and technologies are designed and developed in a way that makes it possible for people with disabilities to use them. Accessibility also benefits people without disabilities for example people with temporary disabilities or situational disabilities.
Accessibility helps people perceive, understand and interact with our digital products and it takes auditory, cognitive, neurological, physical, speech and visual limitations into consideration.

But accessibility is also a mindset and a change in the way we think and talk about digital products. It is a cultural change that cannot be made by one team only. We, therefore, urge all our colleagues as well as top management to get involved and share awareness about this very important topic. We need your support!
why-is-accessibility-important?: One of the main myths about accessibility is that it only concerns a small group of people with severe disabilities. This assumption is far from the reality and according to the World Health Organisation (WHO), more than 1 billion people are living with some level of impairment that affects the way they interact with digital products and access information online.
When our websites, apps and other digital products are designed badly, they create barriers that exclude people and make access to our offerings cumbersome and in some cases not possible at all.
being-inaccessible-is-a-problem: It is an ethical problem when we exclude certain groups of people from our products and services.

It is a legal problem, especially in the US where companies are being sued if they are not accessible, which could have financial consequences in terms of fines as well as affecting the brand negatively.

It is a big business problem - if we choose to exclude a very big group of potential customers because they have a disability.
When we design and build products with accessibility in mind, we not only open up our products and experiences for more people, but our products start to become more human - in the sense that they reflect the diversity of society and become much more flexible and robust to accommodate many different needs and use cases.
